name: Fantasy
description: Stories set in worlds with magical rules or supernatural forces, emphasizing wonder, mythic stakes, and transformative journeys.

nodes.label: Fantasy Promise
nodes.definition: The audience is promised entry into a world where the impossible is real — magic, mythic forces, or supernatural elements exist and matter — and where wonder, transformation, and mythic-scale stakes drive the story.
exit_conditions: Core constraints are established that fulfill this promise
failure_modes: Magical world is dull or generic
Fantasy elements are window dressing with no narrative function
signals_in_text: Early introduction of a world that operates differently from reality
Sense of wonder or awe at the impossible

nodes.label: Magic/Supernatural Must Have Rules
nodes.definition: The magical or supernatural elements must operate according to consistent internal rules — they may be mysterious but they cannot be arbitrary. Magic must have costs, limits, or conditions.
exit_conditions: Audience understands what magic can and cannot do, even if the full system is not explained
typical_variants: Hybrid (hard for protagonist, soft for world)
failure_modes: Magic solves any problem with no cost
Rules change to suit the plot
Magic has no limits or conditions
signals_in_text: Characters reference costs or conditions of magic
Magic fails or has unintended consequences
Audience can predict what magic will and won't do

nodes.label: World Must Be Internally Coherent
nodes.definition: The fantasy world — its geography, cultures, history, politics, and ecology — must be internally consistent. The presence of magic should have visible consequences on how society works.
exit_conditions: World feels like a real place with its own logic, not a collection of cool ideas
failure_modes: World is a grab bag of unrelated ideas
Magic exists but society hasn't adapted to it
Geography and culture are inconsistent
signals_in_text: Details reinforce each other
Characters behave consistently with their culture
Consequences of magic are visible in daily life

nodes.label: Stakes Must Be Mythic or Transformative
nodes.definition: Fantasy stakes should carry a sense of grandeur or fundamental transformation — the fate of a world, the balance of cosmic forces, or a profound personal metamorphosis that changes the nature of things.
exit_conditions: Stakes feel larger or deeper than ordinary conflict
failure_modes: Stakes are mundane despite a magical setting
Grand stakes are announced but never felt
signals_in_text: Prophecies, ancient evils, or cosmic forces referenced
Character's journey has significance beyond the personal
The world itself may change based on the outcome

nodes.label: Sense of Wonder Must Be Present
nodes.definition: Fantasy must evoke wonder — awe at the impossible, delight in the strange, or reverence for the mythic. The audience should feel that entering this world is itself a reward.
exit_conditions: At least some scenes evoke genuine wonder or awe
failure_modes: Magic is treated as mundane
World is described but not evoked
Nothing feels wondrous despite being impossible
signals_in_text: Descriptive passages that invite the reader to see and feel the impossible
Characters (especially newcomers) react with awe
Moments of beauty or terror that could only exist in this world

nodes.label: Epic/High Fantasy
nodes.definition: Fantasy set in a fully realized secondary world with world-spanning stakes, large casts, and a struggle between forces of fundamental significance (good vs. evil, order vs. chaos).
exit_conditions: Story spans significant geography and involves forces larger than any individual
failure_modes: Grand scope but no personal emotional stakes
World is vast but shallow
signals_in_text: Maps, multiple cultures, deep history
Large cast with diverse motivations
Fate of nations or worlds hangs in the balance

nodes.label: Urban Fantasy
nodes.definition: Fantasy set in a contemporary or near-contemporary city where magic coexists with modern life — often hidden from ordinary people.
entry_conditions: Setting is a recognizable modern city
exit_conditions: Magic and modernity create friction and possibility
failure_modes: Modern setting but magic has no interaction with modern life
City is generic backdrop rather than active element
signals_in_text: Familiar city landmarks alongside magical elements
Characters navigate both mundane and magical rules
Technology and magic interact or conflict

nodes.label: Dark Fantasy
nodes.definition: Fantasy that emphasizes horror, moral ambiguity, and the terrible cost of magic — wonder is mixed with dread, and the magical world is as dangerous as it is marvelous.
entry_conditions: Tone is dark, stakes include corruption or horror
exit_conditions: Magic is dangerous, morality is ambiguous, and victory comes at genuine cost
failure_modes: Darkness is purely aesthetic — no moral weight
Becomes horror without the fantasy sense of wonder
signals_in_text: Magic has terrible costs
Moral choices are genuinely difficult
Beauty and horror coexist

nodes.label: Mythic/Fairy Tale Fantasy
nodes.definition: Fantasy that draws directly on myth, folklore, or fairy tale structures — archetypal characters, symbolic landscapes, and stories with the logic of dreams rather than realism.
exit_conditions: Story operates on symbolic and archetypal levels
failure_modes: Mythic elements are decorative rather than structural
Story is a simple retelling without adding depth
signals_in_text: Threes, quests, transformations
Symbolic landscapes (dark forest, crystal tower)
Characters represent archetypes

nodes.label: Magic Has Visible Cost
nodes.definition: The use of magic must carry a price — physical exhaustion, moral corruption, sacrificed memories, shortened lifespan, or some other tangible cost that prevents magic from being a free solution.
exit_conditions: Characters visibly pay for using magic
failure_modes: Magic is free and unlimited
Cost is mentioned but never enforced
signals_in_text: Characters weigh whether to use magic
Aftermath of magic use shows the price
Powerful magic requires proportionally greater cost

nodes.label: World Operates by Its Own Logic
nodes.definition: The fantasy world's institutions, economies, technologies, and social structures must reflect the presence of magic — a world with healing magic would have different medicine; a world with teleportation would have different trade routes.
exit_conditions: Society visibly adapts to the existence of magic
failure_modes: Medieval European setting with magic bolted on
Society ignores the implications of its own magic
signals_in_text: Institutions reference magic in their structure
Characters use magic in daily life, not just in crises
Economic and political systems reflect magical capabilities

nodes.label: Boundary Between Mundane and Magical
nodes.definition: The story must define the boundary between the ordinary and the magical — whether it's a geographical border, a hidden world, a portal, or simply the difference between those who can see magic and those who can't.
exit_conditions: Audience understands where the magical begins and the ordinary ends
failure_modes: Boundary is undefined — magic is everywhere without differentiation
Boundary exists but has no narrative significance
signals_in_text: Crossing moments where characters enter or leave the magical space
Rules change when boundary is crossed
Characters from different sides of the boundary clash

nodes.label: World Entry / Wonder Establishment Scene
nodes.definition: A scene that establishes the fantasy world and evokes wonder — the first time the audience (and often the protagonist) encounters the magical reality.
exit_conditions: Audience has entered the fantasy world and felt its wonder
failure_modes: World entry is flat and unimpressive
Magic appears without any sense of wonder
signals_in_text: Descriptive richness increases
Character reacts with awe or fear
Rules of the ordinary world visibly change

nodes.label: Magic Under Pressure Scene
nodes.definition: A scene where magic is used under significant pressure — revealing both its power and its cost — and the outcome demonstrates the magic system's rules in action.
exit_conditions: Audience understands what magic can do and what it costs through demonstration
failure_modes: Magic works perfectly with no tension
Cost is hand-waved away
signals_in_text: Character pays a visible price for magic use
Outcome depends on established magical rules
Stakes are high enough that magic's limitations matter

nodes.label: Mythic Climax / Transformation Scene
nodes.definition: The climactic scene where the story's mythic stakes are resolved — through a final magical confrontation, a transformation, or a choice that determines the fate of the world or the protagonist's fundamental nature.
entry_conditions: All escalation has led to this point
exit_conditions: Mythic stakes are resolved and the world/protagonist is fundamentally changed
failure_modes: Climax is resolved by mundane means in a magical world
Magical solution appears from nowhere (deus ex machina)
signals_in_text: Maximum magical intensity
Everything established comes together
World or protagonist is permanently altered

nodes.label: Tone of Wonder and Grandeur
nodes.definition: Fantasy requires a sense of the numinous — the tone must convey that the world is bigger, older, stranger, and more marvelous than ordinary life.
exit_conditions: Language and description evoke wonder, awe, or enchantment
failure_modes: Tone is flat and utilitarian despite magical content
Wonder is asserted but not evoked
signals_in_text: Rich descriptive language for magical elements
Scale and age of the world conveyed through detail
Emotional responses to the impossible

nodes.label: Deus Ex Machina Magic
nodes.definition: Fantasy fails when magic solves problems through previously unknown abilities, rule violations, or arbitrary power — when the solution couldn't have been predicted from established rules.
entry_conditions: Climactic or high-stakes magical resolution occurs
exit_conditions: Resolution uses only previously established magical rules and capabilities
typical_variants: New power appears at the last moment
Established limits are broken without cost
failure_modes: This node IS the failure mode — its presence indicates a genre violation
signals_in_text: Audience's reaction is 'where did that come from?' rather than 'of course!'
Solution contradicts established magical rules

edges.label: Promise requires consistent magic system
edges.effects_on_stakes: Magic creates opportunities and constraints that define the conflict
edges.effects_on_character: Characters operate within magical rules that shape their choices
common_alternatives: Hard magic (fully explicit) vs. soft magic (mysterious but consistent)
anti_patterns: Magic with no discernible rules or consistency

edges.label: Promise requires coherent world
edges.effects_on_stakes: World consistency makes consequences predictable and meaningful
edges.effects_on_character: Characters are shaped by their world's rules and culture
common_alternatives: Deep worldbuilding vs. minimal but consistent worldbuilding
anti_patterns: World is a collection of unrelated cool ideas

edges.label: Promise requires mythic stakes
edges.effects_on_stakes: Stakes are grander or deeper than ordinary life
edges.effects_on_character: Characters' actions have significance beyond the personal
common_alternatives: World-scale stakes vs. deeply personal transformation
anti_patterns: Mundane stakes in a magical world

edges.label: Promise requires sense of wonder
edges.effects_on_stakes: Wonder makes the world worth fighting for
edges.effects_on_character: Characters' encounters with the magical are emotionally charged
common_alternatives: Awe-inspiring wonder vs. dark sublime vs. enchanting whimsy
anti_patterns: Magical world feels ordinary and unimpressive

edges.label: Promise requires wonder tone
edges.effects_on_stakes: Tone communicates that this world is extraordinary
edges.effects_on_character: Characters exist in a world of grandeur and possibility
common_alternatives: Majestic vs. dark vs. whimsical vs. unsettling
anti_patterns: Flat, utilitarian prose for a magical world

edges.label: Magic system at world-spanning scale
preconditions: Magic operates at a scale affecting nations and civilizations
edges.effects_on_stakes: Magic determines the fate of worlds
edges.effects_on_character: Characters wield or face magic of immense power
anti_patterns: Grand scope but magic is trivial

edges.label: Magic system hidden in modern world
preconditions: Magic exists within a recognizable contemporary setting
edges.effects_on_stakes: Magic must be hidden from or reconciled with the modern world
edges.effects_on_character: Characters navigate between magical and mundane identities
anti_patterns: Modern setting but magic has no interaction with modernity

edges.label: Magic system with terrible costs and moral ambiguity
preconditions: Magic is dangerous, corrupting, or horrifying
edges.effects_on_stakes: Using magic may be as dangerous as the threats it fights
edges.effects_on_character: Characters face moral corruption through magical power
anti_patterns: Dark aesthetic but magic is safe and consequence-free

edges.label: Magic system as fairy tale/mythic logic
preconditions: Magic operates by symbolic, archetypal rules
anti_patterns: Uses mythic trappings but lacks mythic logic

edges.label: Epic scope requires world that operates by its own logic
edges.effects_on_stakes: World logic creates strategic possibilities and constraints
edges.effects_on_character: Characters are embedded in cultures shaped by magic
anti_patterns: Epic scope but generic medieval Europe setting

edges.label: Urban fantasy requires boundary between worlds
edges.effects_on_character: Characters must navigate between ordinary and magical worlds
anti_patterns: No boundary — magic is openly known in the modern world without consequence

edges.label: Dark fantasy requires visible magical costs
edges.effects_on_stakes: Magic use is itself a source of danger and moral compromise
edges.effects_on_character: Characters are changed and corrupted by the magic they use
anti_patterns: Dark aesthetic but magic is free and safe

edges.label: Mythic fantasy requires threshold between worlds
edges.effects_on_stakes: Crossing into the mythic realm changes the rules
anti_patterns: Mythic elements but no threshold or transformation

edges.label: Boundary crossing creates world entry scene
preconditions: Boundary between mundane and magical established
edges.effects_on_stakes: Audience enters the fantasy world alongside the character
anti_patterns: Boundary crossed without any sense of wonder or change

edges.label: Magic costs create high-stakes magical scenes
edges.effects_on_stakes: Using magic is a decision with real consequences
edges.effects_on_character: Character must choose when magic is worth the price
anti_patterns: Magic used freely without any visible cost

edges.label: World logic creates magical pressure points
preconditions: World logic and magic system established
edges.effects_on_stakes: Magical conflict is shaped by the world's rules
edges.effects_on_character: Character must use the world's logic creatively
anti_patterns: Magic used outside the world's established logic

edges.label: Magic tested builds to mythic climax
preconditions: Magic has been used and its costs demonstrated
edges.effects_on_stakes: Climax requires maximum magical commitment at maximum cost
edges.effects_on_character: Character faces the ultimate magical choice
anti_patterns: Climax resolved without engaging the magic system

edges.label: Arbitrary magic violates the genre contract
preconditions: Magic resolves a problem using previously unknown or contradictory rules
edges.effects_on_stakes: Stakes become meaningless if magic can do anything
anti_patterns: This edge IS the anti-pattern — it describes what to avoid
